description: Grade 12 track — Limits and Continuity, Differentiation, Applications of Derivatives, and Integration (AP Calculus AB/BC level).

lessons.subject: Limits
lessons.subtopic: Epsilon-delta intuition and one-sided limits
lessons.lesson_goal: Student understands the formal epsilon-delta definition at an intuitive level and can evaluate one-sided limits
lessons.formula_spoken: For every epsilon greater than zero there exists delta greater than zero such that if x is within delta of a then f of x is within epsilon of L
lessons.narration_style: precise but accessible, epsilon-delta as zooming
lessons.seo_title: Formal Limit Definition — Epsilon Delta Explained Visually
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The rigorous definition behind every limit
lessons.real_world_hook: Software that controls self-driving cars must guarantee that errors stay below a threshold ε whenever sensor uncertainty is below δ. This is epsilon-delta made physical.
lessons.concept_intuition: Epsilon is the tolerance on output; delta is the tolerance on input. The limit exists if for any output tolerance you give me, I can find an input tolerance that works.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw f(x) near a. Draw a horizontal band of height 2ε around L. Find the corresponding vertical band of width 2δ. Show that as ε shrinks, δ can be adjusted to match.
lessons.common_mistake: Thinking epsilon and delta are specific numbers. They are universal guarantees — the statement must hold for ANY epsilon, no matter how small.

lessons.subject: Limits
lessons.subtopic: Three conditions for continuity and types of discontinuity
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can test a function for continuity at a point using the three-condition checklist and classify discontinuities
lessons.formula_spoken: f is continuous at a if and only if the limit of f as x approaches a equals f of a
lessons.narration_style: checklist-driven, with visual examples of each discontinuity type
lessons.seo_title: Continuity in Calculus — 3 Conditions and Types of Discontinuity
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Can you draw it without lifting your pen?
lessons.real_world_hook: A thermostat that jumps abruptly from OFF to ON has a jump discontinuity. Engineers prefer smooth (continuous) control systems that change gradually.
lessons.concept_intuition: Three conditions must hold: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) the limit exists, (3) they are equal. Violating any one creates a discontinuity.
lessons.visual_hints: Show four graphs: continuous, removable hole, jump discontinuity, infinite discontinuity. Identify which condition fails for each.
lessons.common_mistake: Assuming a function is continuous just because it has no vertical asymptotes. Holes and jumps are also discontinuities.

lessons.subject: Limits
lessons.subtopic: Statement, conditions, and applications
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can state the IVT, verify its conditions, and use it to prove the existence of roots
lessons.key_formula: \text{If } f \text{ is continuous on }[a,b]\text{ and }f(a)<k<f(b),\text{ then }\exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=k
lessons.formula_spoken: If f is continuous on a closed interval and k is any value between f of a and f of b, then there exists a c between a and b where f equals k
lessons.narration_style: existence proof, concrete and visual
lessons.seo_title: Intermediate Value Theorem — Proof of Root Existence
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Why every continuous function must cross zero
lessons.real_world_hook: A car travels from 0 mph to 60 mph. At some exact moment, it was doing exactly 42 mph. You might not know when — but IVT guarantees it happened.
lessons.concept_intuition: A continuous function cannot jump over a value. If it is below k at one end and above k at the other, it must cross k somewhere in between.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw a continuous curve from (a, f(a)) to (b, f(b)). Draw a horizontal line at k. Show that the curve must cross it.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting the continuity requirement. IVT fails for discontinuous functions — they can jump over values.

lessons.subject: Limits
lessons.subtopic: Proof and application of lim(sinx/x)=1
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can evaluate limits involving sin(x)/x and (1-cosx)/x using the standard results
lessons.formula_spoken: The limit of sine x over x as x approaches zero is 1; the limit of 1 minus cosine x over x as x approaches zero is 0
lessons.narration_style: geometric squeeze argument, then formula use
lessons.seo_title: Special Trig Limits — sin(x)/x = 1 Proof and Applications
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Why sin(x)/x equals EXACTLY 1 at zero
lessons.real_world_hook: Signal processing uses sin(x)/x (the sinc function) everywhere in digital audio filters. The fact that its value at zero is exactly 1 is what makes filters work.
lessons.concept_intuition: For very small x in radians, sin(x) ≈ x. So the ratio sin(x)/x approaches 1. The geometric squeeze proof makes this precise using area comparisons.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw a unit circle sector. Show area of triangle < area of sector < area of outer triangle. Squeeze to derive the limit. Then apply to evaluate other limits using substitution.
lessons.common_mistake: Using this limit when x is in degrees. The limit sin(x)/x=1 only holds when x is in RADIANS.

lessons.subject: Limits
lessons.subtopic: Evaluating 0/0 and ∞/∞ indeterminate forms
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can apply L'Hôpital's rule to evaluate limits with 0/0 or ∞/∞ indeterminate forms
lessons.formula_spoken: If the limit gives 0 over 0 or infinity over infinity, then the limit equals the limit of f prime over g prime
lessons.narration_style: systematic, always checking the condition first
lessons.seo_title: L'Hopital's Rule — Evaluating Indeterminate Limits
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The calculus trick for 0/0 limits
lessons.real_world_hook: Economists computing marginal utility at boundary cases constantly encounter 0/0 forms. L'Hôpital's rule gives the economically meaningful answer.
lessons.concept_intuition: When both numerator and denominator vanish, compare their rates of vanishing. Taking derivatives measures those rates. The ratio of rates gives the limit.
lessons.visual_hints: Three-step banner: CHECK (is it 0/0 or ∞/∞?), DIFFERENTIATE (top and bottom separately), EVALUATE (substitute). Show this for two examples.
lessons.common_mistake: Differentiating the whole fraction as a quotient. L'Hôpital's rule says differentiate numerator and denominator SEPARATELY — do not use the quotient rule.

lessons.subject: Differentiation
lessons.subtopic: Definition as a limit, slope of the tangent line
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can state the limit definition of the derivative and interpret it as the instantaneous rate of change
lessons.prerequisite: Limits, slope of a line, function notation
lessons.formula_spoken: f prime of x equals the limit as h approaches zero of f of x plus h minus f of x, all divided by h
lessons.narration_style: secant to tangent transition, building intuition visually
lessons.seo_title: The Derivative — Limit Definition and Instantaneous Rate of Change
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Slope at a single POINT — how?
lessons.real_world_hook: Your phone's step counter measures instantaneous speed between two GPS pings. As the time gap shrinks toward zero, you get the true instantaneous velocity — the derivative.
lessons.concept_intuition: Draw a secant line between two points. Bring them closer and closer together. In the limit, the secant becomes the tangent — and its slope is the derivative.
lessons.visual_hints: Animate a secant line with two points on a curve. As the second point slides toward the first, show the secant line rotating into the tangent. Label h shrinking to zero.
lessons.common_mistake: Canceling h before expanding f(x+h). Always expand first, collect like terms, then cancel h.

lessons.subject: Differentiation
lessons.subtopic: Power rule, constant rule, sum and difference rules
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can differentiate any polynomial using power, constant, and sum/difference rules instantly
lessons.formula_spoken: The derivative of x to the n is n times x to the n minus 1; the derivative of any constant is zero
lessons.narration_style: rule-focused, rapid examples building confidence
lessons.seo_title: Differentiation Rules — Power Rule Sum Rule Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Differentiate ANY polynomial instantly
lessons.real_world_hook: Engineers calculating the velocity of a projectile from its position equation x(t)=5t³−2t²+t use the power rule 60 times per second in simulation software.
lessons.concept_intuition: The power rule says: bring the exponent down in front, then subtract 1 from the exponent. That's it. Constants vanish because flat lines have zero slope.
lessons.visual_hints: Table of functions and derivatives side by side. Show the pattern: xⁿ → nxⁿ⁻¹. Highlight that the exponent becomes the coefficient and decrements.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting that the derivative of a constant times a function is the constant times the derivative: d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x).

lessons.subject: Differentiation
lessons.subtopic: Differentiating a product of two functions
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can apply the product rule to differentiate any product of two differentiable functions
lessons.formula_spoken: The derivative of u times v equals u prime times v plus u times v prime
lessons.narration_style: mnemonic-driven: first-times-derivative-of-second plus second-times-derivative-of-first
lessons.seo_title: Product Rule — How to Differentiate Products of Functions
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Why you CANNOT just multiply derivatives
lessons.real_world_hook: Physics often models force as pressure×area. When both change with position, the product rule gives the instantaneous change in force correctly.
lessons.concept_intuition: When both factors change, you account for: the first factor changing while the second holds steady, plus the second factor changing while the first holds steady.
lessons.visual_hints: Geometric derivation: show a rectangle with sides u and v. Change both by small amounts. Area change = u·Δv + v·Δu + ΔuΔv. As Δ→0, last term vanishes.
lessons.common_mistake: Multiplying the derivatives: (uv)' ≠ u'v'. This is the most common calculus error for new students.

lessons.subject: Differentiation
lessons.subtopic: Differentiating a ratio of two functions
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can apply the quotient rule correctly using the low-d-high minus high-d-low mnemonic
lessons.formula_spoken: The derivative of u over v equals u prime v minus u v prime, all divided by v squared
lessons.narration_style: mnemonic-focused: low-d-high minus high-d-low over low squared
lessons.seo_title: Quotient Rule — Differentiating Fractions of Functions
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Low-D-High minus High-D-Low trick
lessons.real_world_hook: Efficiency (output/input) in engineering changes as both quantities vary. The quotient rule gives the exact rate of change of efficiency.
lessons.concept_intuition: LO d(HI) minus HI d(LO), all over LO squared. Memorise this phrase, never forget quotient rule again.
lessons.visual_hints: Write the formula with 'LO' and 'HI' labels on the board. Work through a full example labelling each piece. Show that forgetting the minus sign gives the wrong answer.
lessons.common_mistake: Reversing the order: it is u'v MINUS uv', not uv' MINUS u'v. The sign matters.

worked_example: f'(x)=\frac{2x\cos x-x^2(-\sin x)}{\cos^2 x}=\frac{2x\cos x+x^2\sin x}{\cos^2 x}

lessons.subject: Differentiation
lessons.subtopic: Differentiating composite functions
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can identify and apply the chain rule to differentiate composite functions
lessons.prerequisite: Composite functions, basic differentiation, product rule
lessons.formula_spoken: The derivative of f of g of x equals f prime of g of x, times g prime of x — outside derivative times inside derivative
lessons.narration_style: outside-inside, peel the layers like an onion
lessons.seo_title: Chain Rule — Differentiating Composite Functions Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Peel the function like an onion
lessons.real_world_hook: A sound wave's intensity depends on pressure, which depends on position, which depends on time. Differentiating this chain of dependencies uses the chain rule — three times.
lessons.concept_intuition: Work from outside to inside: differentiate the outer function (leaving the inner alone), then multiply by the derivative of the inner function.
lessons.visual_hints: Label outer function (f) and inner function (g) in different colors. Step 1: differentiate outer, keep inner. Step 2: multiply by derivative of inner. Build complexity gradually.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting the inside derivative multiplier. (sin(x²))' = cos(x²)·2x, not just cos(x²).

lessons.subject: Differentiation
lessons.topic: Derivatives of Trig and Exponential Functions
lessons.subtopic: sin, cos, tan, eˣ, ln x derivatives
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can state and apply all standard derivatives of trig, exponential, and logarithmic functions
lessons.prerequisite: Chain rule, trig functions, exponential functions
lessons.formula_spoken: The derivative of sine x is cosine x; the derivative of e to the x is e to the x; the derivative of ln x is 1 over x
lessons.narration_style: reference table then application
lessons.seo_title: Derivatives of Trig Exponential Log Functions — Full Reference
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Every derivative rule you need on one board
lessons.real_world_hook: Euler's number e is its own derivative — it grows at its own rate. This makes it the natural base for modelling anything growing continuously: money, bacteria, radioactive decay.
lessons.concept_intuition: eˣ is special because its derivative equals itself. sinx and cosx cycle through each other as derivatives. ln x differentiates to the clean simple 1/x.
lessons.visual_hints: One full-board reference table. Animate the cycle sin→cos→-sin→-cos→sin. Show that eˣ is unchanged by differentiation with a graph.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting the chain rule when the argument is not plain x: d/dx[sin(3x)] = cos(3x)·3 = 3cos(3x), not just cos(3x).

lessons.subject: Differentiation
lessons.subtopic: Differentiating equations where y is implicit in x
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can differentiate implicit equations and find dy/dx without solving explicitly for y
lessons.formula_spoken: The derivative of y to the n with respect to x is n times y to the n minus 1 times dy dx — the chain rule for y
lessons.narration_style: systematic: differentiate both sides, isolate dy/dx
lessons.seo_title: Implicit Differentiation — Finding dy/dx Without Solving for y
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Differentiate a circle — without solving for y
lessons.real_world_hook: The equation of a circle x²+y²=r² cannot be solved for y as a single function. Implicit differentiation finds the slope of the tangent at any point without splitting into two halves.
lessons.concept_intuition: Treat y as a function of x. Whenever you differentiate a y term, multiply by dy/dx (chain rule). Then collect all dy/dx terms and solve.
lessons.visual_hints: Differentiate x²+y²=25 step by step. Show 2x + 2y(dy/dx) = 0. Isolate dy/dx = -x/y. Show evaluating the slope at a specific point.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting to include dy/dx when differentiating y terms. Every y term gets a dy/dx multiplier — the chain rule is always active.

lessons.subject: Differentiation
lessons.subtopic: Using implicit differentiation with respect to time
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can set up and solve related rates problems using implicit differentiation with time as the variable
lessons.prerequisite: Implicit differentiation, chain rule, word problem setup
lessons.formula_spoken: The rate of change of A with respect to time equals the rate of change of A with respect to r, times the rate of change of r with respect to time
lessons.narration_style: four-step process: draw, label, relate, differentiate
lessons.seo_title: Related Rates — Step by Step Method with Examples
lessons.thumbnail_angle: How fast is the water level RISING?
lessons.real_world_hook: A spherical balloon is being inflated at 10 cm³/s. How fast is the radius increasing when the radius is 5 cm? Related rates answer this in three steps.
lessons.concept_intuition: When two quantities are related by an equation, differentiating both sides with respect to time connects their rates of change.
lessons.visual_hints: Four-step framework on the board: (1) Draw diagram. (2) Label known and unknown rates. (3) Write the geometric relationship. (4) Differentiate with respect to t and solve.
lessons.common_mistake: Plugging in specific values BEFORE differentiating. Always differentiate the general equation first, then substitute the given values.

lessons.subject: Applications of Derivatives
lessons.subtopic: Finding critical points and classifying maxima and minima
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can find critical points of a function and identify them as local maxima, minima, or neither
lessons.key_formula: f'(c)=0 \text{ or } f'(c)=\text{DNE} \Rightarrow c \text{ is a critical point}
lessons.formula_spoken: A critical point occurs where the derivative equals zero or does not exist
lessons.narration_style: visual and analytical, connecting graph features to derivative signs
lessons.seo_title: Critical Points — Finding Local Max and Min of Functions
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Where does a function peak or valley?
lessons.real_world_hook: A drone flying a curved path reaches its maximum altitude exactly where vertical velocity becomes zero — the derivative condition for a critical point.
lessons.concept_intuition: At a peak (maximum) or valley (minimum), the slope is zero — the tangent is flat. Critical points are where f'=0 or f' is undefined.
lessons.visual_hints: Plot a cubic function. Mark the maximum and minimum. Show derivative at each: flat tangent lines. Draw f'(x) below — it crosses zero at each critical point.
lessons.common_mistake: Assuming every critical point is a maximum or minimum. A critical point could also be a saddle point (inflection) where the function neither peaks nor valleys.

lessons.subject: Applications of Derivatives
lessons.subtopic: Using sign change of f' to classify critical points
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can use the first derivative test to classify every critical point as local max, local min, or neither
lessons.prerequisite: Critical points, sign analysis of polynomials
lessons.formula_spoken: If f prime changes from positive to negative at c, then c is a local maximum; from negative to positive means a local minimum
lessons.narration_style: sign chart method, systematic and clear
lessons.seo_title: First Derivative Test — Classify Max Min from Sign of f'
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The sign chart trick — local max or min?
lessons.real_world_hook: An aircraft's altitude increases (f'>0) then decreases (f'<0). The moment f' changes sign is the peak — the first derivative test identifies it precisely.
lessons.concept_intuition: If f' goes from + to − at c, the function was rising then falling — it peaked at c. If f' goes from − to +, the function was falling then rising — it valleyed at c.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw a number line for x. Mark critical points. Test f' in each interval. Assign + or − signs. Read off the classification from sign changes.
lessons.common_mistake: Testing only one side of a critical point. You must check the sign of f' on BOTH sides to determine the change.

practice: x=0: \text{no sign change} \Rightarrow \text{neither max nor min}

lessons.subject: Applications of Derivatives
lessons.subtopic: Concavity, inflection points, and second derivative test
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can determine concavity using f'', find inflection points, and apply the second derivative test to classify critical points
lessons.formula_spoken: If f double prime at c is positive, c is a local minimum (concave up); if negative, c is a local maximum (concave down)
lessons.narration_style: bowl vs hill analogy
lessons.seo_title: Second Derivative Test and Concavity — Inflection Points
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Concave UP or DOWN — the second derivative tells you
lessons.real_world_hook: A road curves up like a bowl (concave up) or arches like a hill (concave down). The inflection point is where it switches — engineers mark it on blueprints using f''=0.
lessons.concept_intuition: Concave up (f''>0) means the slope is increasing — like the inside of a bowl. Concave down (f''<0) means the slope is decreasing — like a hill. Inflection is where it switches.
lessons.visual_hints: Show a cubic with both concave-up and concave-down sections. Mark inflection point where f''=0. Then show that at a minimum the curve is bowl-shaped (f''>0).
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing f''=0 automatically means inflection point. It is a candidate — you must verify the SIGN of f'' changes at that point.

practice: g''(0)=0 \text{ but no sign change} \Rightarrow \text{not inflection}

lessons.subject: Applications of Derivatives
lessons.subtopic: Finding absolute maximum and minimum in real-world problems
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can translate a word problem into a calculus optimization and find the optimal value
lessons.prerequisite: Critical points, first/second derivative test, domain restrictions
lessons.key_formula: \text{Find critical points, check endpoints, compare } f \text{ values}
lessons.formula_spoken: Set the derivative equal to zero, find critical points, evaluate at critical points and endpoints, then compare to find the maximum or minimum
lessons.narration_style: five-step recipe: read, draw, express, differentiate, solve
lessons.seo_title: Optimization in Calculus — Max and Min Word Problems
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Maximize profit with ONE derivative
lessons.real_world_hook: Amazon minimizes packaging material cost for every box. Given a fixed volume, the minimum surface area box is found by optimization calculus — saving millions in cardboard annually.
lessons.concept_intuition: Optimization always has one quantity to maximize/minimize and a constraint. Express the objective function in one variable using the constraint, then differentiate.
lessons.visual_hints: Five-step banner. Classic problem: maximize area of rectangle with fixed perimeter. Set up A(x)=x(P/2−x), take derivative, set to zero, find x.
lessons.common_mistake: Having two variables in the objective function without eliminating one using the constraint. Always reduce to a single-variable function first.

practice: \text{Express h in terms of x, differentiate S, set to zero}

lessons.subject: Applications of Derivatives
lessons.subtopic: Statement, conditions, and applications of MVT
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can state MVT, verify its conditions, and find the c guaranteed by the theorem
lessons.formula_spoken: There exists a c between a and b where the instantaneous rate of change equals the average rate of change over the interval
lessons.narration_style: geometric intuition then algebraic application
lessons.seo_title: Mean Value Theorem — Statement Proof and Applications
lessons.thumbnail_angle: A secret point where the slope EQUALS the average
lessons.real_world_hook: If you drove 120 miles in 2 hours, your average speed was 60 mph. MVT guarantees there was at least one exact instant where your speedometer read exactly 60 mph.
lessons.concept_intuition: The MVT says: somewhere between a and b, the tangent line must be exactly parallel to the secant line joining the endpoints. The instantaneous slope equals the average slope.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw a smooth curve from (a,f(a)) to (b,f(b)). Draw the secant line. Slide a parallel tangent line until it touches the curve — that contact point is c.
lessons.common_mistake: Applying MVT without checking both conditions: (1) continuous on [a,b] and (2) differentiable on (a,b). Both are required.

lessons.subject: Applications of Derivatives
lessons.subtopic: Using f, f', f'' together to sketch accurate graphs
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can sketch a complete accurate graph using domain, intercepts, critical points, concavity, and asymptotes
lessons.prerequisite: Critical points, concavity, limits at infinity
lessons.formula_spoken: Use the derivative to find increasing/decreasing intervals and local extrema; use the second derivative to find concavity and inflection points
lessons.narration_style: systematic six-step checklist
lessons.seo_title: Curve Sketching Using Calculus — Complete Step by Step Method
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Draw ANY function accurately with calculus
lessons.real_world_hook: Before graphing calculators, engineers sketched every curve by hand using exactly this method. Understanding what the curve does mathematically is more powerful than plotting points.
lessons.concept_intuition: Think of the graph as a story: where does it start, where does it go, where does it peak, where does it bend, where does it end? f, f', and f'' each answer one chapter.
lessons.visual_hints: Six-step checklist on the board: (1) Domain. (2) Intercepts. (3) Asymptotes. (4) f' sign chart (increasing/decreasing, critical points). (5) f'' sign chart (concavity, inflection). (6) Sketch.
lessons.common_mistake: Skipping asymptotes or limits at infinity — these determine the far-left and far-right behaviour and are essential for a complete sketch.

practice: \text{Use sign charts for }f'\text{ and }f''\text{ to sketch the full curve}

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Reversing differentiation — indefinite integrals
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can find antiderivatives of basic functions and understand why the constant of integration +C is necessary
lessons.formula_spoken: The integral of x to the n is x to the n plus 1, divided by n plus 1, plus C
lessons.narration_style: reverse engineering from differentiation
lessons.seo_title: Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals — Power Rule for Integration
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Reverse the derivative — find the original function
lessons.real_world_hook: Given velocity v(t)=3t², find position x(t). Integrate velocity to get position: x(t)=t³+C. The +C is your starting position — needed because multiple starting points give the same velocity.
lessons.concept_intuition: Integration undoes differentiation: raise the power by 1 and divide by the new power. The +C accounts for all functions that differ only by a constant but share the same derivative.
lessons.visual_hints: Show differentiation and integration as two arrows (forward and back). Table of basic integrals matching the derivative table from earlier lessons.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting +C. Without it, the answer is incomplete — there are infinitely many antiderivatives, differing only by a constant.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Area under a curve as a limit of rectangles
lessons.lesson_goal: Student understands the definite integral as a limit of Riemann sums and can interpret it as signed area
lessons.formula_spoken: The definite integral from a to b is the limit as n approaches infinity of the sum of f at sample points times the width delta x
lessons.narration_style: building rectangles under the curve, narrowing the width
lessons.seo_title: Riemann Sums and Definite Integrals — Area Under a Curve
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Infinite rectangles give the EXACT area
lessons.real_world_hook: The total distance a car travels equals the area under the velocity-time graph. Using infinitely thin time slices — Riemann sums — gives the exact distance.
lessons.concept_intuition: Divide the area under f(x) into n rectangles of equal width Δx. Sum their areas. As n→∞ and rectangles get infinitely thin, the sum converges to the exact area.
lessons.visual_hints: Animate: start with 4 rectangles, then 8, then 16, then 64. Show the approximation improving. Final limit label: the definite integral.
lessons.common_mistake: Thinking the definite integral always equals positive area. It is SIGNED area — regions below the x-axis contribute negative values.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: FTC Part 1 and Part 2 — connecting derivatives and integrals
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can state both parts of FTC and use them to evaluate definite integrals and differentiate integral functions
lessons.formula_spoken: The definite integral from a to b of f equals F of b minus F of a, where F is any antiderivative of f
lessons.narration_style: the bridge between two halves of calculus
lessons.seo_title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus — FTC Part 1 and 2 Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The theorem that connects ALL of calculus
lessons.real_world_hook: Newton and Leibniz independently discovered this theorem — that differentiation and integration are inverse operations. This single theorem unified two centuries of mathematics.
lessons.concept_intuition: FTC Part 2: to find the area under f, find any antiderivative F and compute F(b)−F(a). No limits, no sums — just plug in two numbers and subtract.
lessons.visual_hints: Show FTC Part 2 as the evaluation formula. Show FTC Part 1 (d/dx of an integral with variable limit = the integrand). Connect them on a single slide.
lessons.common_mistake: Writing ∫f(x)dx = F(b)−F(a) without the limits. The definite integral needs specific limits; the indefinite integral has +C.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Reversing the chain rule for integration
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can apply u-substitution to evaluate integrals of composite functions
lessons.formula_spoken: Substitute u for the inner function; du equals g prime of x dx; this transforms the integral into a simpler one in u
lessons.narration_style: four-step process: choose u, find du, substitute, integrate
lessons.seo_title: U-Substitution — Integration Technique Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The reverse chain rule for integrals
lessons.real_world_hook: Physics integrals for work done by a variable force almost always require substitution. Without u-sub, these problems are unsolvable by basic antiderivative rules.
lessons.concept_intuition: U-substitution is the chain rule run backwards. Spot the inner function, let it equal u, compute du, rewrite everything in u, integrate, then substitute back.
lessons.visual_hints: Four-step banner. Show ∫2x(x²+1)⁵dx step by step: choose u=x²+1, du=2xdx, substitute to get ∫u⁵du, integrate to u⁶/6, substitute back.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting to replace dx with du/g'(x). All x-terms must be eliminated before integrating in u.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Reversing the product rule for integration
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can apply integration by parts to integrate products of functions and use LIATE to choose u
lessons.formula_spoken: The integral of u dv equals u times v minus the integral of v du
lessons.narration_style: LIATE mnemonic, methodical application
lessons.seo_title: Integration by Parts — LIATE Method with Full Examples
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Integrate x times sin(x) — impossible without this
lessons.real_world_hook: Calculating the center of mass of a non-uniform rod requires integrating x·density(x)dx. Integration by parts handles this exactly.
lessons.concept_intuition: Integration by parts reverses the product rule. Use LIATE to pick u: Logarithm, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential — pick u from earlier in the list.
lessons.visual_hints: Derive the formula from d(uv)/dx = u'v + uv'. Integrate both sides. Show LIATE chart. Work a complete example with all steps labeled.
lessons.common_mistake: Choosing u and dv incorrectly — if dv is hard to integrate, swap. The whole point is that ∫v du should be easier than the original.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Integrating the difference of two functions
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can set up and evaluate the area between two curves, identifying which function is on top over the interval
lessons.prerequisite: Definite integrals, FTC, solving equations for intersection points
lessons.formula_spoken: Area equals the integral from a to b of the top function minus the bottom function
lessons.narration_style: setup-focused, drawing the region first
lessons.seo_title: Area Between Two Curves — Setup and Integration Method
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Find the area trapped between two graphs
lessons.real_world_hook: Urban planners calculate land area between a road and a river (two curves) to determine how much property lies in a flood zone.
lessons.concept_intuition: The area between two curves is the area under the top curve minus the area under the bottom curve. Find intersection points for the limits, identify top and bottom, integrate the difference.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw two curves with shaded region between them. Label f(x) (top) and g(x) (bottom). Mark intersection points a and b. Show the integral setup.
lessons.common_mistake: Not finding the intersection points first — using wrong limits gives the wrong answer. Always solve f(x)=g(x) to find the bounds.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.topic: Volume by Disk and Washer Method
lessons.subtopic: Rotating regions around the x-axis
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can set up and evaluate volumes of solids of revolution using the disk and washer methods
lessons.prerequisite: Area under a curve, definite integrals, circle area formula
lessons.formula_spoken: Volume equals pi times the integral from a to b of f of x squared dx — each thin disk has radius f of x
lessons.narration_style: 3D visualization built from 2D regions
lessons.seo_title: Volume of Revolution — Disk and Washer Method
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Spin a curve to make a 3D solid
lessons.real_world_hook: A pottery wheel spins clay into a symmetric 3D vase. The cross-section curve defines the shape. Integration using the disk method calculates the exact volume of clay used.
lessons.concept_intuition: Rotate a region around the x-axis. Each thin vertical strip becomes a disk (or washer). The disk has radius f(x), area πf(x)², and thickness dx. Integrate to sum infinitely many.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the 2D region. Show it being rotated around the x-axis. Show a single thin disk extracted. Label radius and thickness. Write the integral.
lessons.common_mistake: Using diameter instead of radius. The area of a disk is π r² — always use the function value as the RADIUS.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Integrals over infinite intervals and integrands with vertical asymptotes
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can evaluate improper integrals by converting to limits and determine convergence or divergence
lessons.prerequisite: Definite integrals, limits at infinity, FTC
lessons.formula_spoken: An improper integral over an infinite interval is the limit of the definite integral as the upper bound goes to infinity
lessons.narration_style: limiting process, two types shown clearly
lessons.seo_title: Improper Integrals — Convergence and Divergence with Examples
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Can you integrate to INFINITY?
lessons.real_world_hook: The probability that a radioactive atom has NOT decayed by time t involves ∫ₜ^∞ λe^{−λx}dx. This improper integral converges — proving the total probability equals 1.
lessons.concept_intuition: Replace the infinite limit with a variable t, integrate normally, then take the limit as t→∞. If the limit exists (finite), the integral converges. Otherwise it diverges.
lessons.visual_hints: Two cases: infinite upper limit (use t→∞) and vertical asymptote inside (split and use one-sided limit). Show each with a graph of the area filling in.
lessons.common_mistake: Evaluating at ∞ directly without the limit. ∞ is not a number — it is a limiting process.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Separable ODEs and slope fields
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can identify and solve separable first-order differential equations and interpret slope fields
lessons.formula_spoken: Separate variables: all y terms and dy go to one side, all x terms and dx go to the other, then integrate both sides
lessons.narration_style: separation technique with geometric slope-field interpretation
lessons.seo_title: Intro to Differential Equations — Separable ODEs and Slope Fields
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Equations where the unknown is a FUNCTION
lessons.real_world_hook: Population growth rate is proportional to population: dP/dt = kP. Solving this separable ODE gives P(t)=P₀eᵏᵗ — the exponential growth model used by every epidemiologist.
lessons.concept_intuition: A differential equation involves a function and its derivative. Separable equations can be split into one side with only y and the other with only x, then integrated separately.
lessons.visual_hints: Show a slope field grid for dy/dx=x. Sketch solution curves threading through the field. Then solve the equation analytically and verify.
lessons.common_mistake: Not including +C and not applying an initial condition to find the particular solution.

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Common AP Calculus question patterns and time management
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can solve the most common AP Calculus free-response patterns under timed conditions
lessons.key_formula: \text{Five high-frequency AP patterns: limit, derivative, integral, ODE, optimisation}
lessons.formula_spoken: The five most common AP Calculus patterns are limits, derivatives, definite integrals, differential equations, and optimization
lessons.narration_style: exam-focused, rapid pattern identification
lessons.seo_title: AP Calculus Exam Strategy — 5 High Frequency Question Types
lessons.thumbnail_angle: 5 AP Calc patterns that appear EVERY year
lessons.real_world_hook: The AP Calculus exam is predictable. The same five problem archetypes appear year after year. Today we drill each one with exam timing so you walk in ready for anything.
lessons.concept_intuition: Pattern recognition saves time. See a 0/0 limit → L'Hôpital. See a moving object → position/velocity/acceleration. See a word problem with one unknown → optimization.
lessons.visual_hints: Five-panel board: one panel per pattern. Each shows the trigger words, the setup, and the solution strategy. Run a timer for each example.
lessons.common_mistake: Starting every integral problem by finding an antiderivative. Check first if u-sub, by parts, or direct formula applies. Choosing the wrong method wastes exam time.

worked_example: \text{Pattern 2: FRQ optimization} \to \text{write objective, write constraint, eliminate variable}
practice: \text{Time trial: solve 3 problems in 15 minutes under exam conditions}

lessons.subject: Integration
lessons.subtopic: Taylor series, Maclaurin series, convergence tests
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can write the Maclaurin series for eˣ, sinx, cosx and apply the ratio test for convergence
lessons.prerequisite: All calculus concepts, derivatives, infinite series
lessons.formula_spoken: e to the x equals the sum from n equals zero to infinity of x to the n over n factorial
lessons.narration_style: building series from derivatives, connecting to known functions
lessons.seo_title: Taylor and Maclaurin Series — Power Series for e^x sin cos
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Turn any function into an infinite polynomial
lessons.real_world_hook: Your calculator does not know how to compute sin(0.5) directly. It uses the Maclaurin series to add up 10 terms and get a 15-decimal answer. Every scientific computation uses series.
lessons.concept_intuition: A Taylor series expresses a function as an infinite polynomial using its derivatives at a point. Each successive term corrects the approximation more precisely.
lessons.visual_hints: Show how adding terms of the Maclaurin series for sin x progressively better approximates the sine curve. Overlay the partial sums 1 term, 3 terms, 5 terms on the same axes.
lessons.common_mistake: Assuming all power series converge for all x. Always check the radius of convergence using the ratio test.
